quiz_questions.question_text: ANOVA is used to compare:

options.text: Two proportions
Three or more group means
Two means only
Variances only

quiz_questions.explanation: ANOVA (Analysis of Variance) extends the two-sample t-test to compare means across three or more groups simultaneously.

quiz_questions.question_text: The null hypothesis in one-way ANOVA states:

options.text: All means are different
All group means are equal
Variances differ across groups
At least one mean differs

quiz_questions.explanation: H-null in ANOVA states that all population group means are equal. The alternative states that at least one mean differs.

quiz_questions.question_text: The F-statistic equals:

options.text: MSW / MSB
MSB / MSW
SSB + SSW

quiz_questions.explanation: F = MSB/MSW = between-group variability divided by within-group variability. A large F suggests groups differ more than expected by chance.

quiz_questions.question_text: A large F-value indicates:

options.text: All means are equal
Between-group differences are large relative to within-group variability
The model is incorrect
Sample size is too small

quiz_questions.explanation: A large F means group means differ more than would be expected from random within-group variation alone, suggesting at least one group mean is different.

quiz_questions.question_text: If F is close to 1, this suggests:

options.text: Strong evidence to reject H-null
Between-group and within-group variability are similar, providing little evidence against H-null
The test has failed
All means are definitely different

quiz_questions.explanation: When F is near 1, between-group variation is about the same as within-group variation, suggesting the groups do not differ more than expected by chance.

quiz_questions.question_text: ANOVA assumes:

options.text: Non-normal data
Dependent samples
Independent samples, approximate normality, and equal variances
No assumptions are required

quiz_questions.explanation: Standard one-way ANOVA requires three key assumptions: independent observations, approximately normal distributions within each group, and equal variances (homoscedasticity).

quiz_questions.question_text: Degrees of freedom for MSB in one-way ANOVA with k groups equals:

options.text: k
n - k

quiz_questions.explanation: Between-group degrees of freedom = number of groups minus 1 = k - 1.

quiz_questions.question_text: Degrees of freedom for MSW in one-way ANOVA with k groups and n total observations equals:

options.text: n - k
k

quiz_questions.explanation: Within-group degrees of freedom = total observations minus number of groups = n - k.

quiz_questions.question_text: SST = SSB + SSW means:

options.text: Total variation equals between-group plus within-group variation
Sum of means equals the total
F = SSB / SSW
Degrees of freedom add up to n

quiz_questions.explanation: Total variability (SST) is partitioned into two components: variation between group means (SSB) and variation within groups (SSW).

quiz_questions.question_text: If ANOVA yields a p-value of 0.001, this means:

options.text: Fail to reject H-null
Strong evidence that at least one group mean differs
All means are definitely identical
The F-statistic equals 0

quiz_questions.explanation: A very small p-value (0.001) provides strong evidence against the null hypothesis that all group means are equal.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does MSB (Mean Square Between) measure?

options.text: Individual data spread within each group
How much the group means differ from the overall grand mean
The residual error
The total variation in the data

quiz_questions.explanation: MSB = SSB / (k-1) quantifies between-group variability. It measures how spread out the group means are from the overall mean.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does MSW (Mean Square Within) measure?

options.text: Differences between group means
The total variation in the dataset
The average variability of observations within each group
The between-group spread

quiz_questions.explanation: MSW = SSW / (n-k) is the pooled within-group variance. It estimates the common variance within groups, assuming equal variances.

quiz_questions.question_text: What are post-hoc tests and when are they used?

options.text: Tests run before ANOVA to check assumptions
Pairwise comparison tests used after ANOVA rejects H-null, to identify which specific groups differ
Tests that replace ANOVA entirely
Tests for checking normality of the data

quiz_questions.explanation: When ANOVA rejects H-null, it only tells us that at least one mean differs but not which one. Post-hoc tests (like Tukey's HSD) perform pairwise comparisons to pinpoint which groups differ.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does Tukey's HSD (Honestly Significant Difference) do?

options.text: Replaces the ANOVA test entirely
Provides pairwise comparisons of all group means while controlling the family-wise error rate
Tests whether data are normally distributed
Tests for equal variances across groups

quiz_questions.explanation: Tukey's HSD compares every pair of group means after a significant ANOVA, adjusting for multiple comparisons to keep the overall Type I error rate at the desired level.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why is running multiple t-tests instead of ANOVA problematic?

options.text: It gives the same result as ANOVA
Multiple pairwise t-tests inflate the overall Type I error rate
t-tests are always more powerful than ANOVA
ANOVA is less accurate than multiple t-tests

quiz_questions.explanation: With k groups, there are k(k-1)/2 possible pairwise comparisons. Each t-test has its own chance of a Type I error, and these compound, greatly inflating the overall false positive rate.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'two-way ANOVA' test?

options.text: One factor only
Two factors and their possible interaction
Three group means only
Only within-group variation

quiz_questions.explanation: Two-way ANOVA examines two independent variables (factors) simultaneously, testing for main effects of each factor and their interaction effect.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'interaction effect' mean in two-way ANOVA?

options.text: The factors are completely independent of each other
The effect of one factor depends on the level of the other factor
No main effects exist in the model
The model is incorrectly specified

quiz_questions.explanation: An interaction means the combined effect of two factors differs from the sum of their individual effects. For example, a drug's effect might depend on whether the patient is male or female.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the ANOVA table display?

options.text: Only the p-value
SS, df, MS, F-statistic, and p-value for each source of variation
Only the group means
Only the regression intercept

quiz_questions.explanation: The ANOVA table organizes all key statistics: sum of squares (SS), degrees of freedom (df), mean squares (MS), the F-statistic, and the p-value for between-group and within-group sources.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'homogeneity of variance' mean in ANOVA?

options.text: All group means must be equal
The population variances of all groups are assumed to be equal
The sample sizes must be equal
The data must be normally distributed

quiz_questions.explanation: Homogeneity of variance (also called homoscedasticity) is the assumption that all groups have the same population variance. Levene's test can be used to check this assumption.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'eta-squared' measure in ANOVA?

options.text: The p-value of the F-test
The proportion of total variance explained by the group membership (effect size)
The number of groups in the analysis
The within-group standard deviation

quiz_questions.explanation: Eta-squared = SSB/SST represents the proportion of total variance accounted for by group differences. It serves as an effect size measure for ANOVA.

quiz_questions.question_text: A psychologist tests three therapy methods (CBT, medication, combined) on depression scores. With 20 patients per group (60 total) and k = 3 groups, how many degrees of freedom does MSB have?
quiz_questions.explanation: df(between) = k - 1 = 3 - 1 = 2. The within-group df would be n - k = 60 - 3 = 57.

quiz_questions.question_text: A farmer tests four fertilizers on crop yield. The ANOVA table shows SSB = 450, SSW = 900, with k = 4 groups and n = 40 total plants. What is the F-statistic?

A company compares employee satisfaction across five departments. After ANOVA yields F = 4.32 with p = 0.003, Tukey's HSD reveals that only the Marketing and IT departments differ significantly. What can the company conclude?

options.text: All five departments have different satisfaction levels
Only Marketing and IT have significantly different mean satisfaction scores; the other departments do not differ significantly from each other
The ANOVA must be wrong because only two groups differ
Post-hoc tests should not have been performed

quiz_questions.explanation: ANOVA's rejection of H-null means at least one pair differs. Tukey's HSD identified the specific pair (Marketing vs. IT). It is common for ANOVA to reject H-null even when only one pair of groups drives the difference.

quiz_questions.question_text: A researcher studies the effect of exercise type (running, swimming, cycling) and diet (low-carb, balanced) on weight loss using two-way ANOVA. The interaction between exercise and diet is significant (p = 0.02). What does this mean?

options.text: Exercise type and diet have no effect on weight loss
The effect of exercise type on weight loss depends on the diet; for example, running may produce more weight loss with low-carb but not with balanced diet
Exercise and diet each independently affect weight loss with no connection
The model is invalid and should be discarded

quiz_questions.explanation: A significant interaction means the effect of one factor depends on the level of the other. The best exercise type for weight loss may vary depending on which diet a person follows.

quiz_questions.question_text: A teacher compares test scores across four sections of the same course. ANOVA gives F = 1.12 with p = 0.35. At alpha = 0.05, what should the teacher conclude?

options.text: At least one section has significantly different scores
There is not enough evidence to conclude that mean scores differ across sections
All sections have exactly the same mean score
Post-hoc tests should be performed

quiz_questions.explanation: Since p = 0.35 > alpha = 0.05, we fail to reject H-null. F = 1.12 is close to 1, suggesting little difference between sections. Post-hoc tests are not warranted when ANOVA fails to reject.

quiz_questions.question_text: A nutritionist compares calorie intake across three age groups. She has 15, 18, and 12 participants in the groups respectively (n = 45 total). If SSB = 12,000 and SSW = 36,000, what is the F-statistic?
A botanist measures plant growth under three light conditions. One group has a clearly non-normal distribution with a small sample of 8 plants. What should the botanist consider?

options.text: Proceed with ANOVA since it requires no assumptions
Use a nonparametric alternative like the Kruskal-Wallis test
Remove the non-normal group from the analysis
Use a chi-square test instead

quiz_questions.explanation: ANOVA assumes approximate normality, and with a small sample (n = 8), the test is not robust to violations. The Kruskal-Wallis test is a nonparametric alternative that does not require normality.

quiz_questions.question_text: An ANOVA with k = 4 groups and n = 60 yields SSB = 300 and SST = 1500. What is eta-squared, and how would you interpret it?

options.text: Eta-squared = 0.80; group membership explains 80% of the variance
Eta-squared = 0.20; group membership explains 20% of the total variance
Eta-squared = 0.50; half the variance is explained
Eta-squared = 5.0; this is a very large effect

quiz_questions.explanation: Eta-squared = SSB/SST = 300/1500 = 0.20. This means 20% of the total variance in the outcome is explained by group membership, which is a medium-to-large effect in many fields.

quiz_questions.question_text: A pharmaceutical company tests three drug dosages (low, medium, high) on blood pressure reduction. ANOVA yields p = 0.008. Tukey's HSD shows: Low vs. Medium (p = 0.42), Low vs. High (p = 0.005), Medium vs. High (p = 0.03). Which pairs differ significantly at alpha = 0.05?

options.text: All three pairs differ significantly
Low vs. High and Medium vs. High differ significantly, but Low vs. Medium does not
Only Low vs. High differs significantly
No pairs differ significantly

quiz_questions.explanation: Low vs. High (p = 0.005 < 0.05) and Medium vs. High (p = 0.03 < 0.05) are both significant. Low vs. Medium (p = 0.42 > 0.05) is not. The high dose produces significantly different results from both other doses.

quiz_questions.question_text: A sports scientist measures reaction time for athletes in three sports (soccer, basketball, tennis) at two times of day (morning, evening) using two-way ANOVA. Results show: Sport main effect (p = 0.001), Time main effect (p = 0.15), Interaction (p = 0.72). What should the scientist report?

options.text: Sport type and time of day both significantly affect reaction time
Only sport type significantly affects reaction time; time of day and the interaction are not significant
The interaction is significant, so main effects cannot be interpreted
None of the effects are statistically significant

quiz_questions.explanation: At alpha = 0.05: Sport (p = 0.001) is significant, Time (p = 0.15) is not, and the interaction (p = 0.72) is not. The sport an athlete plays matters for reaction time, but time of day does not, and the effect of sport does not depend on time of day.
